textInfo.title: Moderate Inflammation Marker (hs-CRP)
textInfo.medicalDescription: hs-CRP level is 2.4 mg/l, which falls into the 'mittleres KHK Risiko' (medium coronary heart disease risk) category of 1.0 - 3.0 mg/l. This indicates a moderate level of systemic inflammation.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP) level is 2.4 mg/l. This test measures inflammation in your body. Your result falls into a category associated with a moderate risk of future cardiovascular events. Reducing inflammation through lifestyle factors like diet, exercise, and stress management can be beneficial.

textInfo.title: Elevated Pancreatic Amylase
textInfo.medicalDescription: Pancreatic amylase level is elevated at 64 U/l, above the reference range of <53 U/l. This may indicate pancreatic stress or inflammation.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your level of pancreatic amylase, an enzyme made by the pancreas to help digest carbohydrates, is slightly elevated. This can sometimes be a sign of pancreas inflammation (pancreatitis) or other issues with the pancreas. Since the elevation is minor, it may not be significant, but it's something to be aware of and potentially discuss with your doctor.

textInfo.title: Elevated HOMA-IR (Insulin Resistance)
textInfo.medicalDescription: HOMA-Index is elevated at 1.88, with the reference range being 0-1. This indicates early insulin resistance.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your HOMA-IR score, a measure of insulin resistance, is elevated at 1.88 (normal is below 1). This suggests that your body's cells are not responding as well as they should to insulin, a condition known as insulin resistance. This is an early warning sign for metabolic issues, including pre-diabetes and type 2 diabetes, and can often be improved with diet and lifestyle changes.

textInfo.title: Slightly Elevated LDL Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: LDL Cholesterol is elevated at 3.2 mmol/l, which is above the reference range of <3.1 mmol/l.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your LDL cholesterol, often called 'bad' cholesterol, is slightly elevated. High levels of LDL can contribute to the buildup of plaque in your arteries, increasing the risk of heart disease. Lifestyle changes like a heart-healthy diet and regular physical activity can help lower LDL levels.

textInfo.title: Elevated Non-HDL Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: Non-HDL Cholesterol level is 3.9 mmol/l. This is above the target for moderate risk (<3.4 mmol/l), indicating an increased concentration of atherogenic lipoproteins.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your Non-HDL cholesterol, which includes all the 'bad' types of cholesterol, is elevated. This is a key indicator of your risk for heart disease. Managing this level through diet, exercise, and if necessary, medication, is important for cardiovascular health.

textInfo.title: Reported Thyroid Condition and Weight Gain
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports thyroid underfunction, significant weight gain in the last year, and difficulty losing weight. This is consistent with their reported use of Letrox (Levothyroxine).
textInfo.originalDescription: Is there anything else you feel would be helpful for us to know about your health or lifestyle?: I have theroid undefunction and have gained a lot of weight last year.. i can’t lose weight so its frustrating! Thats all i know
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: You mentioned having an underactive thyroid ('theroid undefunction') and experiencing significant weight gain over the past year with difficulty losing it. This information is important for understanding your overall health and metabolism.

textInfo.title: Thyroid Medication Use
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports taking Letrox 50, a brand name for Levothyroxine, used for treating hypothyroidism.
textInfo.originalDescription: Are you currently taking any vitamins, minerals, or other dietary supplements?: Yes, Supplement Name: Letrox , Dosage: 50
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: You are taking Letrox with a dosage of 50, which is a medication commonly used for treating an underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism).

textInfo.title: Elevated Monocytes
textInfo.medicalDescription: Monocytes value is 10.0 % which is above the reference range of 2.0-9.5 %.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your blood test shows a slightly elevated level of monocytes, a type of white blood cell. This can be a sign that your body is fighting off an infection, or it can be related to inflammation or other conditions. Since the elevation is minor, it is often temporary and not a cause for major concern, but it's worth monitoring.

textInfo.title: Family History of Brain Cancer
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports a family history of brain cancer in their mother, who was diagnosed at age 44.
textInfo.originalDescription: Has anyone in your close family members diagnosed with one of the following diseases? Brain Cancer. Who: Mother. At what age: 44
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: You've indicated a family history of brain cancer. Your mother was diagnosed at the age of 44.

textInfo.title: Borderline High Total Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: Total cholesterol is at the upper limit of the reference range (<5.0 mmol/l). This is considered a borderline high value.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your total cholesterol level is at the upper end of the normal range. While not considered high, it's a good idea to monitor this value and consider lifestyle adjustments, such as diet and exercise, to maintain healthy cholesterol levels and support your cardiovascular health.
